Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running your Flask app as a server versus when running a CLI command. This can be achieved by modifying the way your CLI commands are registered or by detecting the context in which the CLI is being executed. However, the standard `@app.cli.command` decorator is tightly coupled with the Flask app instance, which is created using the `create_app` function. Therefore, the CLI commands are only available when the app is fully initialized.

To avoid setting an environment variable each time you run a CLI command, you can create a separate entry point or wrapper script that handles the CLI commands differently. This allows you to bypass the need for an environment variable by detecting whether the CLI is being run in the context of the Flask app or not.

You can also modify the `create_app` function to accept parameters that determine the mode of operation, such as whether it's running as a server or a CLI command. This can be done by passing an argument to `create_app` when initializing the app, but this requires careful handling to ensure that the app is not reinitialized multiple times.

In the case of CLI commands, you can use the `flask` command-line interface to run your commands. However, by default, the CLI is only available when the app is running in the Flask development server. Therefore, you can't directly run CLI commands without the app being initialized.

If you want to run CLI commands without starting the Flask app, you can use a custom script that initializes the app in a different way. This script can then call the CLI commands directly, bypassing the need for the environment variable.

To make this more seamless, you can define a separate `create_cli_app` function that initializes the app with specific configurations for CLI operations. This function can be used when running the CLI commands instead of the standard `create_app`.

You can also use the `flask` command-line interface to run your CLI commands, but you need to ensure that the app is properly initialized before any CLI commands are executed. This can be done by using a custom script that loads the app and then runs the CLI commands.

In your `create_app` function, you can check the context in which the app is being used. For example, you can determine whether the app is being run as a server or as a CLI command by checking the presence of certain attributes or by inspecting the command-line arguments.
